section.title: Set a Guard over My Mouth
section.sectionRationale: A Davidic lament framing prayer itself as incense and evening sacrifice, this psalm turns from crying for rescue outward, Psalm 140, to guarding the heart and mouth within, its welcome of a righteous friend's rebuke as oil for the head modeling the humility that keeps a believer from sliding into the very evil he prays against.
section.thesis: David asks the LORD not only to rescue him from external violence but to guard his own mouth and heart from evil, welcoming a righteous friend's rebuke as kindness while trusting God alone to spring the traps of evildoers, so that he may pass by safely while they fall into their own snares.
section.frameNote: Three movements: a fragrant, urgent cry for God to hear, verses 1-2; the plea for a guarded mouth and heart, including welcome of righteous rebuke, verses 3-7; and eyes fixed on God alone for refuge from hidden snares, verses 8-10.

units.label: Let my prayer be set before You like incense

verses.text: I call upon You, O LORD; come quickly to me. Hear my voice when I call to You.
May my prayer be set before You like {{ketoret|incense}}; my uplifted hands, like the evening offering.

units.label: Set a guard over my mouth

verses.text: Set a guard, O LORD, over my mouth; keep watch at the door of my lips.
Do not let my heart be drawn to any evil thing or take part in works of wickedness with men who do iniquity; let me not feast on their delicacies.
Let the righteous man strike me; let his {{tokhechah|rebuke}} be an act of loving devotion. It is oil for my head; let me not refuse it. For my prayer is ever against the deeds of the wicked.
When their rulers are thrown down from the cliffs, the people will listen to my words, for they are pleasant.
As when one plows and breaks up the soil, so our bones have been scattered at the mouth of Sheol.

units.label: My eyes are fixed on You

verses.text: But my eyes are fixed on You, O GOD the Lord. In You I seek refuge; do not leave my soul defenseless.
Keep me from the snares they have laid for me, and from the lures of evildoers.
Let the wicked fall into their own nets, while I pass by in safety.

ketoret.gloss: Incense, burned morning and evening at the temple, Exodus 30:7-8. David asks that his prayer, offered far from the sanctuary, be received by God with the same acceptance as the fragrant, appointed incense offering.

tokhechah.gloss: Rebuke, correction, reproof. David welcomes the tokhechah of a righteous friend as an act of loving devotion, likening it to healing oil poured on the head, a striking picture of correction received as kindness rather than resented as attack.

basic.context: David prays with the language of temple worship, incense and evening offering, even while apparently far from the sanctuary, asking not only for rescue from enemies but for a guarded mouth and heart, and welcoming the correction of a righteous friend as a kindness rather than an insult.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes David's own urgent, worshipful prayer life amid danger from without and temptation from within; it prescribes framing all prayer as an offering to God, guarding one's own speech and heart, and receiving godly rebuke as grace rather than as a burden to be refused.
basic.originalLanguages: David asks that his prayer be received like ketoret, incense, the fragrant offering burned morning and evening at the temple, Exodus 30:7-8. He welcomes a friend's tokhechah, rebuke, as an act of loving devotion, oil for his head, a correction that heals rather than wounds.
basic.typology: David's prayer offered like incense anticipates the saints' prayers pictured as incense before God's throne, Revelation 5:8, and his welcome of righteous rebuke as oil for the head anticipates Christ's own pattern of receiving no deceit and returning no threats under unjust treatment, 1 Peter 2:22-23.
basic.christ: Christ embodies this psalm's guarded mouth perfectly, committing no sin, no deceit found in His mouth, and when reviled, He did not revile in return, 1 Peter 2:22-23, and He now intercedes for His people, presenting their prayers, like this psalm's incense, acceptable before the Father.
basic.god: God is shown as the one who receives prayer as a fragrant offering, who alone can guard mouth and heart from evil, and who is trusted as refuge from every hidden trap, while human correction, rightly received, becomes one of His means of keeping His people from stumbling.
basic.crossReferences: Proverbs 27:6 states the same principle as verse 5, faithful are the wounds of a friend. Revelation 5:8 pictures the saints' prayers as incense before God's throne, echoing verse 2. 1 Peter 2:22-23 shows Christ's own guarded mouth under unjust attack, the fulfillment of David's plea in verse 3.
basic.oneStory: David's prayer offered like incense, his guarded mouth, and his welcome of a friend's healing rebuke anticipate Christ, whose mouth held no deceit and who now presents His people's prayers acceptably before the Father, teaching every believer both how to pray and how to receive correction as grace.

context.literary: An individual lament structured around a chiastic center at verse 5, moving from invocation, verses 1-2, to personal restraint and welcome of correction, verses 3-5, to a scene of judgment on rulers, verses 6-7, and closing in refuge and reversal, verses 8-10.
context.historical: Attributed to David, many place it during Saul's persecution or Absalom's rebellion, seasons when David was hunted yet refused unholy alliances for survival, and when physical distance from the tabernacle heightened his longing to approach God through prayer as though it were sacrifice.
context.canonical: The second psalm in the cluster of Davidic laments closing Book V, Psalms 140-143, it turns the outward cry for rescue of Psalm 140 inward toward guarding one's own heart and mouth from the same evil being prayed against.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To frame urgent prayer as an acceptable offering to God, to ask for a guarded mouth and heart amid temptation, to model humble reception of a friend's righteous correction, and to entrust final judgment of evildoers to God alone.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific circumstances of danger and temptation; it prescribes the abiding pattern of offering prayer as worship, guarding speech and heart, welcoming godly correction, and trusting God rather than self for both protection and vindication.

debates.question: What does verse 5's, let the righteous man strike me, actually envision?

views.label: A literal or figurative physical rebuke, striking as a vivid picture of how seriously David welcomes correction, even painful correction, from a godly friend
views.case: The parallel line calls it oil for my head, a healing, anointing image, suggesting the strike itself, however severe, is received as a gift rather than an injury.

views.label: A strong verbal rebuke or discipline rather than literal physical violence, using strike as intense figurative language for confrontation
views.case: The Hebrew verb for strike is frequently used figuratively for strong correction or discipline in wisdom literature, and the psalm's focus throughout is on words and hearts rather than physical combat.

debates.bearing: Either way, the verse teaches that godly correction, however forceful it feels, is to be welcomed as loving devotion rather than resented, a needed corrective to pride that refuses all rebuke.

study.originalLanguages: David asks his prayer be received like ketoret, incense, the fragrant offering burned at the temple morning and evening, Exodus 30:7-8. He welcomes a friend's tokhechah, rebuke, as loving devotion, oil for his head, correction that heals rather than wounds.

typology.fulfillment: The saints' prayers pictured as incense before God's throne, Revelation 5:8.

typology.type: Welcome of righteous rebuke as loving devotion, verse 5
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own pattern of receiving no deceit and returning no threats under unjust treatment, 1 Peter 2:22-23.

study.christ: Christ perfectly embodies this psalm's guarded mouth, committing no sin and speaking no deceit, and when reviled He did not revile in return, 1 Peter 2:22-23. He now presents His people's prayers, like this psalm's incense, acceptable before the Father, interceding for them continually.
study.god: God receives prayer as a fragrant offering, alone can guard mouth and heart from evil, and is trusted as refuge from every hidden trap, while godly correction, rightly received, becomes one of His appointed means of keeping His people from stumbling.

crossReferences.note: States the same principle as verse 5, faithful are the wounds of a friend, but deceitful are the kisses of an enemy.
Pictures the saints' prayers as incense before God's throne, echoing verse 2's request that prayer be received like incense.
Describes Christ's own guarded mouth under unjust attack, the fulfillment of David's plea in verse 3.

study.oneStory: David's prayer offered like incense, his guarded mouth, and his welcome of a friend's healing rebuke anticipate Christ, whose mouth held no deceit and who now presents His people's prayers acceptably before the Father, teaching every believer both how to pray and how to receive correction as grace.

sermon.title: Pray for God to Meet You
sermon.why: In this Look at the Book study on Psalm 141:1-4, Piper explores why David prays for God to come, unpacking the psalm's opening plea and its call to guard the mouth, directly serving this passage's structure and themes.

quiz.q: What does David ask concerning his prayer in verse 2?

options.text: That it be set before God like incense, and his lifted hands like the evening offering.
options.note: David borrows temple worship language to frame his prayer as an acceptable offering to God.

options.text: That it be answered only after a long delay for testing.
options.note: The verse asks that prayer be received like a worship offering, not that it be delayed.

options.text: That it be judged according to how eloquent his words are.
options.note: The request is about acceptance as worship, not eloquence of speech.

options.text: That it never be heard by anyone but himself.
options.note: The prayer is directed openly to God as an offering, not hidden from being heard.

quiz.q: What does David ask God to guard in verse 3?

options.text: His mouth, asking the LORD to keep watch at the door of his lips.
options.note: This is the psalm's central request, guarding speech from evil words.

options.text: His house from physical intruders.
options.note: The request concerns his mouth and speech, not his physical dwelling.

options.text: His wealth from being stolen.
options.note: The psalm is not concerned with material possessions here but with guarded speech.

options.text: His army from defeat in battle.
options.note: The request is personal and inward, guarding his mouth, not a request about military forces.

quiz.q: How does David describe the rebuke of a righteous person in verse 5?

options.text: As an act of loving devotion and like oil for his head, something he should not refuse.
options.note: David frames godly correction as a healing kindness rather than an insult to be resisted.

options.text: As a betrayal he must avenge.
options.note: The verse frames rebuke as kindness to welcome, not betrayal to avenge.

options.text: As something to be politely ignored.
options.note: David explicitly says let me not refuse it, welcoming rather than ignoring the correction.

options.text: As proof that the righteous person is actually his enemy.
options.note: The verse specifically calls this rebuke an act of loving devotion, not evidence of enmity.

quiz.q: Where does David say his eyes are fixed in verse 8?

options.text: On God the Lord, in whom he seeks refuge.
options.note: This declaration anchors the psalm's closing confidence in God alone as refuge.

options.text: On his own military strength and strategy.
options.note: David turns his gaze to God, not to self-reliance or strategy.

options.text: On the wealth of his enemies.
options.note: The verse is about trust in God, not envy of enemies' possessions.

options.text: On escaping to a foreign nation for safety.
options.note: David seeks refuge in God directly, not in fleeing to another nation.

quiz.q: What does David expect will happen to the wicked in verse 10?

options.text: That they will fall into their own nets while he passes by safely.
options.note: This reversal, the trapper caught in his own trap, closes the psalm with confident trust in God's justice.

options.text: That they will escape all consequences permanently.
options.note: The verse expects the opposite, that the wicked will fall into the very traps they set.

options.text: That David himself must personally destroy them.
options.note: David entrusts their downfall to their own schemes and to God, not to his own hand.

options.text: That he will join them in their schemes to survive.
options.note: The whole psalm has asked God to keep David from joining in the wicked's schemes, verse 4, not to join them.

questions: David asks that his prayer be received like incense, a fragrant, acceptable offering to God. What would it look like for your prayer life this week to feel less like a duty and more like worship offered to God?
David prays first for a guarded mouth and heart before praying for rescue from his enemies. Where do you most need God to set a guard over your mouth or heart right now?

David welcomes a righteous friend's rebuke as an act of loving devotion, oil for his head. How do you typically respond to correction, and what would it take to receive it as a kindness rather than an attack?
David refuses to feast on the delicacies of those who do wickedness, even while surrounded by danger. What compromises are you being tempted toward for the sake of comfort or safety?

David fixes his eyes on God alone as refuge rather than on his own strength or strategy. What would it mean this week to fix your eyes on God rather than on your own attempts to manage a difficult situation?

translationNotes.note: The evening offering refers to the daily evening sacrifice at the temple, Exodus 29:39, which David's prayer is likened to even though he may have been far from the sanctuary at the time.

When their rulers are thrown down from the cliffs pictures a scene of decisive judgment on those who oppose God, after which David's own words will be recognized by the people as trustworthy and welcome.
Do not leave my soul defenseless carries an alternate rendering, do not pour out my life, both readings expressing David's plea that God not abandon him to his enemies' traps.

No em or en dashes were present in the source text for this psalm. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming John Piper's 'Pray for God to Meet You' Look at the Book study at desiringgod.org, Scripture Psalm 141:1-4.
